# Rayner's Notes

> Personal technical blog of Mingyu Chen (Rayner) — Apache Doris PMC Chair and Co-Founder & VP of Engineering at VeloDB. Long-form writing on data systems, lakehouse architecture, semi-structured data, and the engineering tradeoffs behind open-source analytics databases.

The author has about 12 years of experience building distributed data systems, previously as a Senior R&D Engineer at Baidu and now as co-founder of VeloDB, the company behind the managed offering of Apache Doris. All posts are written in English.

## Posts

- [Native Vector Search in Apache Doris 4.1: 900 QPS at 97% Recall](https://morningman.github.io/posts/doris-41-vector-index/): Apache Doris 4.1 ships native ANN inside an OLAP engine — IVF for tens of millions to a billion vectors, IVF_ON_DISK following SPANN's two-tier design (centroids in memory, posting lists on SSD), and INT8/INT4/PQ quantization stacking on top to reach ~48× compression. Ann Index Only Scan removes the raw-vector column read for top-K queries, taking single-box throughput to 900 QPS at 97% recall on 1M × 768-dim vectors. Structured filters and BM25 fuse with vector recall (RRF) inside one optimizer-planned SQL statement, replacing the post-filter / pre-filter / cross-system stitch the application would otherwise own.
- [Integrating Lance into Doris: Notes from the Rust Side](https://morningman.github.io/posts/doris-lance-rust-integration/): Apache Doris PR #62182 adds a native Lance reader written in Rust and called from the C++ BE. Data crosses the FFI boundary via the Arrow C Data Interface (zero-copy RecordBatch), async stays contained on a single-threaded Tokio with block_on, and Cargo wires into CMake through Corrosion. The 50–80MB binary growth is a one-time cost that future Rust components (delta-rs, iceberg-rust, OpenDAL) amortize on the same FFI plumbing.
- [Doris Segment V3 vs Parquet & Lance: Fixing Footer Metadata Explosion](https://morningman.github.io/posts/segment-v3-vs-open-formats/): Wide tables, ML feature stores, and Variant fields push columnar footers from KB into MB, breaking the "footer is small" assumption columnar formats were built on. Compares three concurrent fixes: Parquet's Flatbuffer proposal (an in-place engine swap that rides inside an extension slot in the old Thrift footer for ecosystem compatibility), Lance (40-byte fixed footer with per-column protobufs and no row groups, possible only because schema and indexes live above), and Doris segment v3 (minimally invasive — externalize ColumnMetaPB to a CMO region and add a path secondary index for Variant). Same problem, three coordinate systems shaped by who controls the readers.
- [Apache Doris 4.1 on Iceberg V3: Running the Full Lakehouse Lifecycle from One SQL Engine](https://morningman.github.io/posts/apache-doris-41-iceberg-v3-lakehouse/): Apache Doris 4.1 adds Iceberg V3 support. UPDATE, DELETE, and MERGE INTO work on Iceberg tables using Deletion Vectors (which collapse Position Delete files into a single Puffin bitmap) and Row Lineage (which gives CDC pipelines a watermark that survives compaction). Covers the design motivation, concrete benchmarks, and a five-minute quick start.
- [Beyond JSON: The Evolution of Variant Data Types in Modern Analytics](https://morningman.github.io/posts/beyond-json-variant-data-types/): JSON has moved from a supporting format to core business data, especially in the AI agent era. Compares how Snowflake (fully managed), Apache Doris (performance-first with fine-grained controls), and Iceberg/Parquet (open standard) implement subcolumn extraction to handle semi-structured data at analytics speeds. Argues that open formats are winning the interoperability layer.
- [How Hard Is It to Add an Index to an Open Format: Lessons from the Apache Iceberg Community](https://morningman.github.io/posts/how-hard-is-it-to-add-an-index-to-an-open-format/): A walkthrough of the design tradeoffs the Apache Iceberg community is navigating as it standardizes secondary indexes. Covers synchronous vs. asynchronous maintenance, metadata placement, snapshot binding, the five index types under discussion (Bloom, B-Tree/Covering, Full-text, Vector, Delete/MOR acceleration), and why building a unified index framework matters more than shipping any specific index first.

## About

- [About the author](https://morningman.github.io/about/): Full bio, professional context, and external links.

## External references

- [Apache Doris](https://doris.apache.org): The open-source real-time analytics and search database the author serves as PMC Chair.
- [VeloDB](https://velodb.io): The company where the author is Co-Founder & VP of Engineering; builds the managed offering of Apache Doris.
- [VeloDB YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/@VeloDB_IO): Tech talks and demos for Apache Doris and related lakehouse topics.
